title: About This Site
text:
This website is a working handbook for data science in the Golden Lab. It is designed for students and collaborators who need practical guidance on:
The site was expanded from the ideas in presentation.qmd, then reorganized into documentation pages so that the material is easier to revisit after onboarding.

title: About This Site
text:
What this site is for
Use this handbook when you need a lab default, a project scaffold, a pointer to the right infrastructure, or a concise explanation of why a workflow recommendation exists.

title: About This Site
text:
What this site is not
This is not a substitute for official FASRC policy, study-specific data use agreements, or project-specific SOPs. When those sources disagree with a local habit, the official requirement wins.

title: About This Site
text:
How to contribute
The best contributions are:

corrections to outdated instructions,
clearer examples,
additions to the data and workflow guides,
and links to lab-specific templates that save other people time.

When in doubt, prefer small improvements and clear examples over long policy text.

title: Collaboration and Support
text:
Version control is not only for backup. It is how the lab understands change, reviews methods, and avoids conflicting local copies of the same project.
The presentation frames Git and GitHub as the tool for traceability, and that is exactly right: commit history should explain what changed, when, and why.

title: Collaboration and Support
text:
Version control is not only for backup. It is how the lab understands change, reviews methods, and avoids conflicting local copies of the same project.
The presentation frames Git and GitHub as the tool for traceability, and that is exactly right: commit history should explain what changed, when, and why.

title: Collaboration and Support
text:
Daily Git habits
Use Git for active work, not just for milestone dumps.

Create the repository early.
Commit small, coherent changes.
Use commit messages that describe the decision or milestone.
Push regularly so the work is visible and recoverable.
Keep data out of Git unless policy clearly allows it.

The NSAPH handbook also recommends pushing code regularly, especially before asking for help or presenting work.1

title: Collaboration and Support
text:
Branches and pull requests
For solo projects, a lightweight branch workflow is still useful:

Make a branch for risky or substantial work.
Open a pull request when the method or output changes meaningfully.
Use review comments to discuss code, assumptions, and documentation gaps.
Merge only when the branch leaves the repository in a reproducible state.

title: Collaboration and Support
section: What a good commit message looks like
text:
What a good commit message looks like
Better:

add first pass of temperature exposure merge
document MCBS variable cleaning assumptions
move repeated model code into helper function

Worse:

updates
stuff
final final version

title: Collaboration and Support
section: How to ask for help effectively
text:
How to ask for help effectively
R for Data Science recommends preparing a minimal reproducible example when you are stuck.2 That is one of the highest-leverage habits students can learn.
When asking the RSE or another lab member for help, include:

the repo or branch,
the exact script or notebook,
the command you ran,
the full error message,
the smallest reproducible example you can produce,
and any data access constraints that prevent direct debugging.

title: Collaboration and Support
section: What to send before a meeting
text:
What to send before a meeting
For technical meetings, send:

the current notebook or report,
a short status note,
open questions,
and links to the relevant code or pull request.

This shifts meetings away from “what happened?” and toward “what decision do we need to make?”

title: Collaboration and Support
text:
Code review checklist
Review for:

reproducibility,
path portability,
data leakage risk,
hidden assumptions,
duplicated code,
and missing documentation.

Do not review only for style.

title: Collaboration and Support
text:
Support ladder
Use this order when possible:

Check the project README and this site.
Search the official docs for the tool you are using.
Reduce the problem to a minimal example.
Open an issue or send a focused help request.
Escalate to synchronous help when the blocker persists.

title: Collaboration and Support
text:
The real goal
The goal is not to make every student a Git expert. The goal is to make the lab’s work inspectable, shareable, and recoverable.

title: Collaboration and Support
text:
Further reading

R for Data Science (2e): Workflow: getting help
NSAPH Handbook: Introduction to NSAPH GitHub
NSAPH Handbook: Workflow Documentation

title: Collaboration and Support
text:
Footnotes

Hadley Wickham, Mine Cetinkaya-Rundel, and Garrett Grolemund, R for Data Science (2e): Workflow: getting help.↩︎

title: Projects and Reproducibility
text:
R for Data Science recommends one project per analysis and treating the project directory as the analysis home.1 That is the cleanest default for lab work too.
A project should contain everything required to understand and rerun the code except restricted data that must stay in approved shared locations.

title: Projects and Reproducibility
text:
R for Data Science recommends one project per analysis and treating the project directory as the analysis home.1 That is the cleanest default for lab work too.
A project should contain everything required to understand and rerun the code except restricted data that must stay in approved shared locations.

title: Projects and Reproducibility
section: Scripts are the source of truth
text:
Scripts are the source of truth
The most important R4DS workflow recommendation is simple: for larger projects and collaboration, the source of truth should be the scripts, because scripts plus data recreate the environment.2
That generalizes beyond R:

in R, keep the workflow in scripts, Quarto notebooks, and package lockfiles,
in Python, keep it in scripts, notebooks, pyproject.toml, requirements.txt, or environment.yml,
and in either language, avoid workflows that depend on an IDE’s memory of prior objects.

title: Projects and Reproducibility
text:
Relative paths only
R4DS is explicit here: once you are inside a project, use relative paths, not absolute paths, because absolute paths hinder sharing.3
Good:
readr::read_csv(here::here("data", "analytic", "cohort.csv"))
Bad:
readr::read_csv("/Users/alice/Desktop/final_data/cohort.csv")

title: Projects and Reproducibility
text:
A practical project layout
project-name/
├── README.md
├── .gitignore
├── data/
├── data-raw/
├── code/
├── notebooks/
├── reports/
├── figures/
├── results/
├── scratch/
└── renv.lock
Suggested meanings:

data/: pointers, symlinks, small metadata, and lightweight project-owned data
data-raw/: import receipts, extraction scripts, or manifests for original inputs
code/: reusable scripts and functions
notebooks/: exploratory and explanatory Quarto notebooks
reports/: rendered outputs for meetings, manuscripts, or handoff
results/: small derived artifacts worth keeping
scratch/: local pointers to transient compute outputs when appropriate

title: Projects and Reproducibility
text:
Notebook versus script versus function
Use a notebook when you need to explain reasoning.
Use a script when you need to run a stable step repeatedly.
Use a function when logic is reused in more than one place.
Use a pipeline when many steps depend on each other and reruns are expensive.

title: Projects and Reproducibility
text:
Environment management
The presentation already points to renv and conda, and that is still the right baseline.
For R:
install.packages("renv")
renv::init()
For Python, commit an environment.yml or pyproject.toml and keep it current.
The goal is not perfection. The goal is that another person, or future you, can reconstruct the environment without guesswork.

title: Projects and Reproducibility
text:
Configuration, not hard-coded infrastructure
If a project depends on shared paths outside the repository, put those paths in configuration.
default:
  analytic_root: "/n/shared/project-data"
  scratch_root: "/n/netscratch/project-name"
Then use that configuration in code instead of repeating infrastructure paths throughout the analysis.

title: Projects and Reproducibility
section: Save outputs from code, not with the mouse
text:
Save outputs from code, not with the mouse
If a figure matters, it should be saved from the script or notebook that created it. R4DS makes this point for figures, but the same logic applies to tables, model objects, and reports: reproducible export beats manual copy-paste every time.

title: Projects and Reproducibility
text:
When to move beyond notebooks
Move to a pipeline or more modular structure when:

the same notebook is rerun many times with small edits,
multiple students touch the same workflow,
the compute cost makes reruns expensive,
or you can no longer tell which cells are still authoritative.

title: Projects and Reproducibility
text:
A strong minimum standard
Every active project should have:

a readable README,
a committed .gitignore,
a current notebook or report,
relative paths,
and an environment specification.

title: Projects and Reproducibility
text:
Further reading

R for Data Science (2e): Workflow: scripts and projects
R for Data Science (2e): Communicate
NSAPH Handbook: How to work on FASSE

title: Projects and Reproducibility
text:

title: Workflow Roadmap
text:
flowchart LR
  A[Question and scope] --> B[Access and infrastructure]
  B --> C[Project scaffold]
  C --> D[Data intake and documentation]
  D --> E[Exploration and notebooks]
  E --> F[Reusable scripts or pipeline]
  F --> G[Review and communication]
  G --> H[Archive and handoff]






The point of the workflow is not bureaucracy. The point is to make a project understandable and rerunnable at every stage.

title: Workflow Roadmap
text:
flowchart LR
  A[Question and scope] --> B[Access and infrastructure]
  B --> C[Project scaffold]
  C --> D[Data intake and documentation]
  D --> E[Exploration and notebooks]
  E --> F[Reusable scripts or pipeline]
  F --> G[Review and communication]
  G --> H[Archive and handoff]






The point of the workflow is not bureaucracy. The point is to make a project understandable and rerunnable at every stage.

title: Workflow Roadmap
text:
1. Question and scope
Start by writing down:

the scientific question,
the unit of analysis,
the key inputs,
the expected outputs,
and the likely compute or security constraints.

If this is not written down, folder structure and code quality will not save the project.

title: Workflow Roadmap
text:
2. Access and infrastructure
Decide where the work belongs before coding heavily:

public or low-risk exploratory work may fit on Cannon or another standard environment,
regulated or contract-restricted work may require FASSE or another secure environment,
and heavy preprocessing or simulation should be designed for scheduled compute, not a laptop session.

Create the project in the environment where it will actually live.

title: Workflow Roadmap
text:
3. Project scaffold
Set up the repository immediately. A good starting point is:
project-name/
├── README.md
├── .gitignore
├── data/
├── data-raw/
├── code/
├── notebooks/
├── reports/
├── figures/
├── results/
└── renv.lock or environment.yml
The NSAPH handbook recommends a very similar repository layout because it separates code, figures, reports, results, and data from the start.1

title: Workflow Roadmap
text:
4. Data intake and documentation
Before analysis scales up, document:

where the authoritative data lives,
who controls access,
whether you should symlink instead of copy,
the update cadence,
key identifiers,
and what cannot leave the secure environment.

If a dataset deserves an analysis, it deserves a data dictionary.

title: Workflow Roadmap
text:
5. Exploration and notebooks
Do early exploration in notebooks or scripts that explain:

what you are testing,
what assumptions you are making,
what looks wrong,
and what should happen next.

R for Data Science treats communication as part of the data science loop, not a final wrapper placed on top of “real” analysis. That is the right model for lab work too.2

title: Workflow Roadmap
text:
6. Reusable scripts or pipeline
Once a workflow stabilizes, move repeated logic out of exploratory notebooks and into:

scripts,
functions,
parameterized reports,
or a pipeline tool such as targets, make, or snakemake.

The trigger is simple: if you expect to rerun it, scale it, or hand it off, it should not live only in an exploratory notebook cell.

title: Workflow Roadmap
text:
7. Review and communication
Every project should be able to produce:

a short README,
one current notebook or report that explains the analysis status,
a reproducible path from data inputs to results,
and a Git history that reflects real milestones.

This makes supervisor meetings, methods review, and cross-training materially easier.

title: Workflow Roadmap
text:
8. Archive and handoff
Before a paper, graduation, or staff transition:

confirm the repo can be cloned and run from scratch,
confirm restricted data are not in Git,
confirm documentation identifies canonical data locations,
render a final report or project website,
and record ownership for future maintenance.

title: Workflow Roadmap
text:
Minimal viable workflow
If you are overwhelmed, do these five things first:

Work on the correct cluster.
Put the analysis in Git.
Use relative paths.
Keep one notebook or report up to date.
Write enough documentation that another lab member could restart the project next week.

title: Workflow Roadmap
text:
Further reading

R for Data Science (2e): Workflow: scripts and projects
R for Data Science (2e): Communicate
NSAPH Handbook: How to work on FASSE
NSAPH Handbook: Workflow Documentation

title: Workflow Roadmap
text:
Footnotes


NSAPH Handbook, How to work on FASSE.↩︎
Hadley Wickham, Mine Cetinkaya-Rundel, and Garrett Grolemund, R for Data Science (2e): Communicate.↩︎

title: Golden Lab Data Workflows
text:
Lab defaults


1. Remote + reproducible beats local + manual
If work matters, it should run on supported infrastructure and be re-runnable from code.


2. Code is the source of truth
Results, figures, and tables are products of the workflow. The lasting record is the code and documentation that created them.


3. Analysis should be narrated
Use notebooks and reports to explain what you did, why you did it, and what should happen next.


4. Robust habits compound
Version control, clean project structure, and minimal documentation reduce technical debt for everyone.

title: Golden Lab Data Workflows
section: What students should be able to do
text:
What students should be able to do
By the end of onboarding, a student should be able to:

Start a project on the correct FASRC system and choose an appropriate storage location.
Create a reproducible project structure with notebooks, scripts, and dependency tracking.
Read shared datasets without making unnecessary copies.
Push analysis code and documentation to GitHub without exposing restricted data.
Ask for help with enough context that someone else can reproduce the problem.

title: Golden Lab Data Workflows
text:
Suggested reading path

Read Start Here for the workflow philosophy and first-week checklist.
Read Workflow Roadmap for the full research lifecycle.
Use the guide pages as reference while you are actively working on a project.
Keep Reference Library open when you need the official docs.

title: Golden Lab Data Workflows
section: This website is itself part of the recommendation
text:
This website is itself part of the recommendation
R for Data Science frames communication as a core part of the data science process, and specifically points readers to Quarto for integrating prose, code, and results. That is the same pattern used here: documentation is not an afterthought, it is part of the workflow.2

title: Golden Lab Data Workflows
text:
Footnotes


If you were looking for the Golden Lab Google Public Site, click here(#TODO FIND THE OLD SITE).↩︎
Hadley Wickham, Mine Cetinkaya-Rundel, and Garrett Grolemund, R for Data Science (2e): Communicate.↩︎

title: Contribution Guide
text: We welcome contributions to the Golden Lab Data Science Handbook! If you have suggestions for improvements, additional content, or corrections, please feel free to submit a pull request or open an issue on our GitHub repository.

title: Contribution Guide
text:
How to Contribute
This site is built using Quarto, which is a powerful publishing and technical writing tool that allows us to create a static website from markdown files that can easily integrate prose, code, and visualizations. If you want to contribute, you will primarily be doing so by editing the markdown files in the repository.

title: Contribution Guide
text:
Getting Started
There are three ways to contribue to this documentation:

Directly on GitHub: You can edit the markdown files directly on GitHub by navigating to the file you want to edit and clicking the pencil icon. This is a quick and easy way to make small changes or corrections.
Locally in Your Own Environment: For larger contributions, you may want to clone the repository to your local environment, make your changes, and then push them back to GitHub. This allows you to test your changes locally before submitting a pull request.


Editing on GitHubEditing Locally


Editing on GitHub is straightforward. Simply navigate to the file you want to edit, click the pencil icon, make your changes, and then commit them directly to the repository. This method is ideal for small edits, such as fixing typos or updating links. However, even for small contributions, it’s best to provide a clear paper trail for what you changed and why, and so we require that all contributions — even small ones — be made through GitHub Issues and Pull Requests1. If this is your first time contributing to an open-source project, this as great low-stakes opportunity to try out this workflow!

Navigate to GitHub. In your browser of choice, go to the GitHub repository: https://github.com/GoldenPlanetaryHealthLab/GoldenPlanetaryHealthLab.github.io
Find “Issues”: Click the Issues tab second from the left.
Open an Issue: Click the green “New Issue” button on the right.
Pick the Documentation Template: Click “🧪 New Data Science Tutorial or Documentation”
Fill Out the Template: A template will appear for you to fill out. Please provide a clear title and description of your contribution, however small, and any relevant links or references. For irrelevant fields, simply write “N/A”.
Assign Yourself: Assign yourself to the issue using the “Assignees” section on the right. This is what will enable us to award your contribution to you in the all-contributors table and give you credit for your work.
Edit the File: Navigate to the file you want to edit, click the pencil icon, and make your proposed changes using markdown, Quarto features, and/or code chunks as needed. You can preview your changes using the “Preview changes” tab to make sure everything looks good.
Commit Your Work: Commit your changes using the dialogue box to the repository. In the commit message, please use the GitHub Issues linking syntax to reference the issue number (e.g., “Fixes #123”) to link your changes to the issue you created. Importantly, make sure to select the option to commit your changes to a new branch and create a pull request. This will automatically open a pull request for your changes, which will be reviewed by the maintainers of the repository.




GitHub Issue Template




Working locally2 is more involved and requires familiarity with git and the command line. However, this is a useful skill to have and is the standard workflow for contributing to open-source scientific projects. Strap in, and let’s get you set up to contribute like a pro!

Create the Issue on GitHub to Work On: Before you start working on your contribution, it’s important to create an issue on GitHub to track your work. This allows the maintainers to review your proposed changes and provides a clear record of your contribution. Navigate to the GitHub repository, click on the “Issues” tab, and create a new issue using the “🧪 New Data Science Tutorial or Documentation” template.
Set up your local environment for git: On FASRC, git comes pre-installed, but in case you can’t find it, type which git. If you get a path back, you’re good to go. If not, load the git module with module load git.
Set Up Your Local Environment for GitHub: GitHub has a handy CLI tool called gh that makes it easy to interact with GitHub from the command line, including creating issues, making Pull Requests, and managing your authentication. To install gh on FASRC, see the instructions here #TODO, and return to this tutorial.

You might be aware that FASRC also has some guidance on working with GitHub here. Frankly, their instructions are a bit dated and their recommendations are less user friendly than just using the gh CLI tool, so we recommend following the instructions above instead of those on FASRC’s website.

Clone the Repository: Find an appropriate directory to work in (since this repository will likely have many individuals contributing to it and very little data or computation, it’s safe to do in your home directory) and run gh clone command to clone the repository to your local environment:

gh repo clone GoldenPlanetaryHealthLab/GoldenPlanetaryHealthLab.github.io
# activate the environment for the repository if applicable

# source .venv/bin/activate # if you have a virtual environment set up for this repository

Create a Local Issue Branch: It’s best practice to create a new branch for your changes, so using the gh CLI tool, create a new branch based on the issue you created in step 1:

gh issue develop [ISSUE NUMBER] --checkout

Make Your Changes: Now that you have your local environment set up and a new branch created, you can make your changes to the markdown files using your preferred text editor or IDE. Be sure to save your and commit your changes frequently, and test them locally if necessary.
Preview Your Changes: If you want to preview your changes locally before submitting a pull request, you can use the quarto preview command. This will start a local server where you can view your changes in a web browser.
Create a Pull Request: Once you’re happy with your changes, you can create a pull request using the gh CLI tool:

gh pr create



GitHub Pull Request In Action

title: Contribution Guide
section: Closing the Issue and Submitting a Pull Request
text:
Closing the Issue and Submitting a Pull Request
To complete the process, review the Pull Request, adding relevant information to the Pull Request description, and then submit it for review. The maintainers will review your changes and may provide feedback or request changes before merging your contribution into the main branch.

title: Contribution Guide
text:
all-contributors Specification (Advanced Users Only)
We use the all-contributors specification to recognize and celebrate the contributions of everyone who has helped with this project. This includes not only code contributions but also documentation, design, and other forms of support e.g. feedback, issue submission and conversation, etc.
You can automate the process of adding contributors by using the all-contributors CLI tool. On Mac, install npm first:
brew install npm node
Then, use the following command to add a contributor:
npm install all-contributors-cli
The project is already initialized with all-contributors, so you can start adding contributors right away. You can add contributors from the command line. For example, to add a contributor with the username octocat who has contributed code and documentation, you would run:
npx all-contributors add octocat code,doc
And then create the contribution table with:
npx all-contributors generate
This will update the README.md file with the new contributor and their contributions. Commit and push this file to GitHub to see the changes reflected in the repository’s README.

title: Contribution Guide
text:
Footnotes


This is a common practice in open-source projects to ensure that all contributions are reviewed and documented properly. And, to your benefit, if you make changes using git your contributions will be automatically tracked and attributed to you, which is important for recognizing your work and giving you credit for your contributions.↩︎
While we use the word, “locally,” here, what we really mean is, “not on GitHub or on the cloud.” Locally can refer to your personal computer, or FASRC (which is what we expect you to use). The key point is that, “locally,” means, “you are directly interfacing with the machine where you will be making the edits.”↩︎

title: Start Here
text:
Most graduate students do not struggle because the science is impossible. They struggle because the workflow is fragile: files live on laptops, results are copied by hand, folders drift out of sync, and the explanation for a key decision is trapped in somebody’s memory.
The goal of this site is to make the robust path the normal path.

title: Start Here
text:
Most graduate students do not struggle because the science is impossible. They struggle because the workflow is fragile: files live on laptops, results are copied by hand, folders drift out of sync, and the explanation for a key decision is trapped in somebody’s memory.
The goal of this site is to make the robust path the normal path.

title: Start Here
text:
The working philosophy
The original presentation reduces to a few durable defaults:

prefer remote and reproducible workflows over local and manual ones,
keep code, not outputs, as the source of truth,
narrate analysis with notebooks and reports,
track change with Git and GitHub,
and invest early in structure so later work gets cheaper.

R for Data Science makes the same argument in a more general form: for larger projects and collaboration, the lasting record of an analysis should be the scripts, because scripts plus data can recreate the environment, while an IDE session alone cannot.1

title: Start Here
text:
Fragile versus robust workflows







Fragile workflow
Robust workflow




Lives on one machine
Lives on supported infrastructure and in version control


Depends on clicking and memory
Depends on scripts, notebooks, and documented steps


Stores only outputs
Stores the method that creates outputs


Breaks when paths, packages, or people change
Survives new machines, new users, and reruns

title: Start Here
section: What “source of truth” means in practice
text:
What “source of truth” means in practice
Treat these artifacts as the core record of a project:

A README that says what the project is and how to run it.
Scripts or notebooks that recreate the analysis.
A lockfile or environment spec such as renv.lock, environment.yml, or requirements.txt.
Data documentation that explains inputs, keys, units, and restrictions.
Git history that shows what changed and why.

Treat these as derived products:

figures,
rendered reports,
slides,
manuscripts,
and intermediate outputs that can be regenerated.

title: Start Here
text:
First-week checklist

Get cluster access and confirm you can log in through the correct FASRC pathway.
Create a repository for your project before the analysis becomes messy.
Create a minimal project scaffold with directories for code, data documentation, figures, reports, and results.
Build one small end-to-end example that reads data, runs one analysis step, and renders one output.
Write down where the authoritative data lives and what restrictions apply.
Push code and documentation to GitHub before asking for help.

title: Start Here
section: What the RSE is helping you do
text:
What the RSE is helping you do
The RSE role is not just “fix code when it breaks.” It is also to help students:

choose the correct compute environment,
avoid avoidable copies of large or regulated data,
standardize project layout,
move from ad hoc scripts to reusable workflows,
and lower the cost of collaboration across the lab.

title: Start Here
section: Common failure modes to avoid early
text:
Common failure modes to avoid early

Running long jobs on a laptop because it feels faster to start.
Hard-coding absolute paths that only work for one person.
Saving important objects only in the interactive environment.
Renaming scripts to final_v2_really_final.
Pushing data to GitHub because the .gitignore was never set up.
Waiting to document the project until after the analysis is already confusing.

title: Start Here
text:
Next pages

Go to Workflow Roadmap for the full lifecycle.
Go to HPC and Infrastructure if you need help deciding where work should run.
Go to Projects and Reproducibility if you are setting up a new analysis repo.

title: Start Here
text:
Further reading

R for Data Science (2e): Workflow: scripts and projects
R for Data Science (2e): Communicate
NSAPH Handbook: Workflow Documentation

title: Start Here
text:
Footnotes


Hadley Wickham, Mine Cetinkaya-Rundel, and Garrett Grolemund, R for Data Science (2e): Workflow: scripts and projects.↩︎

title: HPC and Infrastructure
text:
The presentation starts from a practical point: do the science on infrastructure designed for the science. That usually means remote compute, durable storage, and workflows that can survive browser crashes, laptop failures, or long runtimes.
FASRC’s current recommendation is consistent with that framing: for R users, the recommended starting point is RStudio Server on Open OnDemand.1

title: HPC and Infrastructure
text:
The presentation starts from a practical point: do the science on infrastructure designed for the science. That usually means remote compute, durable storage, and workflows that can survive browser crashes, laptop failures, or long runtimes.
FASRC’s current recommendation is consistent with that framing: for R users, the recommended starting point is RStudio Server on Open OnDemand.1

title: HPC and Infrastructure
text:
Choose the right access path








Need
Best starting point
Why




Interactive R work
Open OnDemand RStudio Server
Fast setup, browser-based, simplest package installation


Interactive shell work
SSH or OOD shell access
Best for CLI tools, Git, file inspection, and job submission


Long or heavy compute
Slurm batch jobs
Reproducible, schedulable, and not tied to your browser session


Secure interactive work
FASSE OOD
Required when data restrictions demand the secure environment



According to the FASRC OOD documentation, the dashboard gives you file browsing, job status, job submission, remote desktops, and interactive apps such as RStudio, Matlab, and Jupyter.2

title: HPC and Infrastructure
text:
Cannon versus FASSE
Use the environment that matches the data and policy requirements:

Cannon is the standard FASRC cluster for general research computing.
FASSE is the secure environment for work that requires stronger data protection.

The NSAPH handbook explicitly distinguishes Cannon from FASSE this way and uses FASSE for regulated data workflows.3

title: HPC and Infrastructure
text:
Recommended pattern for students

Connect to the correct VPN realm.
Start an interactive session for exploration.
Prototype a small slice of the analysis.
Move stable steps into scripts.
Schedule heavier work through Slurm or another batch mechanism.
Keep code and documentation in GitHub; keep restricted data on approved storage.

title: HPC and Infrastructure
section: Storage tiers and what they are for
text:
Storage tiers and what they are for








Storage
Use it for
Do not use it for




Home directory
Small personal config and lightweight project files
Large shared analysis outputs


Shared lab/project storage
Canonical project code and approved shared data access
Personal scratch or duplicate copies


/n/netscratch
High-I/O temporary intermediates
Permanent storage or anything that is not reproducible


GitHub
Code, docs, templates, issues, and small text-based metadata
Raw data, large binaries, or restricted files



FASRC documents /n/netscratch as temporary, high-performance, not backed up, and subject to a 90-day retention policy.4

title: HPC and Infrastructure
text:
FASRC tips that save time

Use Open OnDemand when you want the simplest path into interactive compute.
Use the “My Interactive Sessions” area to reconnect to running browser-based sessions.
Use Chrome or Firefox for OOD; FASRC warns that Safari may not behave well for some apps.
If package installation fails in FASSE RStudio Server, check whether you need to set the FASRC proxy variables first.
Remember that RStudio Server on OOD is convenient, but FASRC notes that module load and sbatch are not available inside that app.

title: HPC and Infrastructure
text:
Practical examples

Start a shell session
ssh username@login.rc.fas.harvard.edu
For FASSE, use:
ssh username@fasselogin.rc.fas.harvard.edu


Use scratch for temporary intermediates
mkdir -p /n/netscratch/<lab-or-project>/<project-name>
Then point temporary outputs there from your scripts instead of filling the repository or home directory.


If FASSE R package installs need a proxy
Sys.setenv(http_proxy = "http://rcproxy.rc.fas.harvard.edu:3128")
Sys.setenv(https_proxy = "http://rcproxy.rc.fas.harvard.edu:3128")

title: HPC and Infrastructure
text:
Decision rule
If the job is slow, large, shared, scheduled, or security-sensitive, it belongs on HPC infrastructure by default.

title: Data Access and Management
text:
Datasets are not just files. They come with:

access controls,
provenance,
update schedules,
variable definitions,
and rules about what can leave the secure environment.

The lab workflow should make all of that visible.

title: Data Access and Management
text:
Datasets are not just files. They come with:

access controls,
provenance,
update schedules,
variable definitions,
and rules about what can leave the secure environment.

The lab workflow should make all of that visible.

title: Data Access and Management
section: Prefer canonical locations over duplicate copies
text:
Prefer canonical locations over duplicate copies
The NSAPH handbook recommends symlinking shared analytic datasets instead of making new copies.1 That is a strong general rule for lab work:

keep the canonical dataset in an approved shared location,
point to it from the project when possible,
and only create project-owned copies when the analysis truly requires them.

title: Data Access and Management
text:
A simple data lifecycle





flowchart LR
  A[Raw source data] --> B[Document source and restrictions]
  B --> C[Create project pointers or symlinks]
  C --> D[Build analytic data reproducibly]
  D --> E[Generate figures, tables, and models]
  E --> F[Archive documentation and outputs]

title: Data Access and Management
text:
Data categories to think about







Category
Typical handling




Public data
Can often be downloaded or scripted directly inside the project


Shared internal data
Reference canonical lab locations and document ownership


Restricted data
Keep in approved secure storage; never push to public Git hosting


Derived analytic data
Rebuild from code when possible; document version and inputs

title: Data Access and Management
text:
Minimum metadata for each dataset
For each important input, record:

dataset name,
canonical path or URL,
data owner or steward,
update cadence,
key identifiers,
units and missing-value conventions,
access restrictions,
and the script that creates downstream analytic tables.

title: Data Access and Management
text:
Data dictionaries and process diagrams
The NSAPH workflow documentation explicitly recommends data diagrams and data dictionaries.2 Both are worth standardizing in the lab:

use a data dictionary when many variables need explanation,
use a flow diagram when multiple sources are joined or filtered,
and store both close to the repository rather than in private notes.

title: Data Access and Management
text:
Raw data should stay raw
Do not edit raw data files in place. Instead:

leave the original input untouched,
write cleaning or harmonization code in code/,
save documented derived outputs to approved locations,
and make sure the transformation path is reproducible.

title: Data Access and Management
text:
Pins, manifests, and lightweight registries
For small derived artifacts, shared models, parameter tables, or published outputs, use a lightweight registry pattern:

pins for R objects or shareable artifacts,
CSV or YAML manifests for file inventories,
and README-based tables for canonical paths and ownership.

This gives you traceability without treating every dataset like application code.

title: Data Access and Management
text:
Git rules for data
Commit:

schemas,
dictionaries,
manifests,
scripts,
notebooks,
and small example data when policy allows it.

Do not commit:

restricted raw data,
large transient intermediates,
files in scratch,
or anything that violates the DUA or institutional policy.

title: Data Access and Management
text:
A .gitignore pattern worth keeping
data/
*.csv
*.parquet
*.fst
*.rds
Refine this by project, but start from the assumption that code and documentation belong in Git while sensitive or bulky data usually do not.

title: Data Access and Management
section: Data access requests should answer these questions
text:
Data access requests should answer these questions

What dataset do you need?
Where is the canonical source?
What approvals or training are required?
What environment are you allowed to use?
Can the data be shared, or only the code?
Who should maintain the access instructions when the project ends?

title: Reference Library
text:
Suggested order for new students

Read Start Here.
Read the R4DS workflow chapters.
Read the FASRC docs page for the environment you will actually use.
Read the relevant NSAPH handbook page that matches your project type.
Return to this site when you need a lab-default answer instead of a generic answer.
